oncurrent and Secure
ia Linked Cross-shard
ement
E, and Jin Zhang, Member, IEEE
However,  to  preserve  security,  existing  permissionless
blockchain sharding systems usually configure large size for
each shard, causing poor transaction concurrency [6], [21],
[23], [29], [35], [46], [47]. In general, under the same network
size, we can increase the number of shards by reducing the
shard size (i.e.,  the number of nodes per shard) to improve
the transaction concurrency [8]. However, arbitrarily reducing
shard size will cause security problems. Specifically, most
existing blockchain sharding systems require that the fraction
of malicious nodes  in each shard be  less  than 1/3 (i.e.,
the fault  tolerance threshold of BFT-typed consensus [12]).
As nodes are typically randomly assigned to shards, merely
reducing shard size can easily result  in a larger fraction of
malicious nodes in certain shards. In this case, these shards are
corrupted, and the malicious nodes can launch attacks (e.g.,
double-spending [2]) to compromise security. Therefore, most
sharding systems have to set large shard size to ensure a neg-
ligible failure probability for each shard. In OmniLedger [29],
a shard must  include 600 nodes when 1/4 of  the network
nodes are malicious. This large shard size undermines intra-
shard consensus efficiency and diminishes the shard count,
consequently limiting transaction concurrency.
In this paper, we aim to boost transaction concurrency for
blockchain sharding securely and efficiently. This is achieved
by proposing a blockchain sharding system named Spiral-
Shard. Unlike most previous works, which require each shard
to be uncorrupted, our intuition is to allow the existence of
some corrupted shards with a  larger  fraction of malicious
nodes. Therefore, the system has more and smaller shards for
higher concurrency. To prevent corrupted shards from com-
promising the system’s security, we design an Linked Cross-
shard Endorsement  (LCE) protocol. Specifically,  shards are
divided into multiple endorsement groups, each belonging to
one group. Each shard must include block headers from other
shards within its blocks to express its endorsement decision.
Blocks within a shard require endorsement in sequence from
all other shards within the same endorsement group to be
finalized securely.
With  the help of  endorsement  groups,  each shard can
securely finalize its blocks, even if there are forks. As a result,
the shard size is reduced without violating security, and the
concurrency is boosted.
However,  it is not straightforward to design the LCE pro-
tocol. We summarize the main challenges as follows.
1). How to maintain low overheads for each shard during

Fig. 1.  Linked Cross-shard Endorsement for Shardi.
cross-shard verification? Our LCE protocol leverages cross-
shard verification and endorsement to secure corrupted shards,
introducing additional  overhead.  For  example,  to validate
blocks from other shards, a straightforward idea is to store
the state of other shards and verify raw transactions, leading
to higher storage, computation, and communication overhead
for each node.

Hence, we need to reduce the overhead of cross-
shard verification and endorsement.
To overcome this considerable overhead, our main idea is
that each shard verifies and ensures its transactions’ validity
by using its own shard’s state (rather than storing the state of
others). On the other hand,  the LCE protocol addresses the
possible forking problem by transmitting block headers across
the shards, reducing overhead. To achieve this goal, we first
observed that when the number of malicious nodes is fewer
than the quorum size (representing the votes needed for BFT-
typed consensus, set as 2/3 of the shard size), blocks with
invalid transactions fail  to amass enough votes during intra-
shard consensus. Hence,  the validity of each block can be
verified by checking the votes in the block header, and ma-
licious nodes cannot manipulate transactions to compromise
safety. Therefore, we limit  the fraction of malicious nodes
in corrupted shards  to be less  than 2/3, guaranteeing the
transaction’s validity. In this case, each shard only needs to
transmit its block headers across shards, and another shard can
address the forking problem and verify the block’s validity via
the block header but not the raw transactions. This mechanism
significantly reduces the overhead for each node.
2). How to preserve safety and liveness in LCE protocol
while some shards are corrupted? In SpiralShard, the safety
indicates that honest nodes agree on the same and valid shard
chain for each shard. Although the validity of blocks can be
verified by checking the votes in the block header, a corrupted
shard may fork its chain to attack safety. Considering that, our
intuition is to leverage an honest shard to endorse the blocks
of corrupted shards. Since such an honest shard will not have
forks,  it will only endorse blocks on one fork branch in the
corrupted shard chain, thus eliminating the forks. However, we
cannot know in advance whether a shard is honest since nodes
are randomly assigned to shards.  Therefore, we establish the
concept of the endorsement group, which comprises multiple
shards (while each shard simultaneously belongs to an indi-

2
vidual endorsement group). Additionally, we fine-tune the size
of the endorsement group to guarantee that each group has at
least one honest shard. Subsequently, we require each shard of
an endorsement group to endorse the blocks of other shards. In
this case, once a corrupted shard receives endorsements from
all shards in its endorsement group, only one fork branch is
selected, eliminating forks and ensuring safety.
However,  in the aforementioned design,  the asynchronous
endorsements of the same corrupted shard by different shards
within the same endorsement group give rise to a particular
challenge. This setup may result in a scenario where various
honest shards endorse distinct blocks of the identical corrupted
shard, thereby preventing any block from accumulating suffi-
cient endorsements to be finalized. This situation threatens the
system’s liveness. We require the shards in an endorsement
group  to conduct  endorsement  sequentially  to  tackle  this
issue. Each shard exclusively endorses the preceding shard
in the endorsement group, following the ascending order of
the shard IDs. Simultaneously, the endorsement from a shard
implies its support for the endorsement decision made by the
preceding shard. Consequently, endorsements from different
shards are linked,  contributing to the finality of  the same
original block. In other words, subsequent honest shards can
only endorse  the endorsements made by preceding honest
ones and cannot contradict them. This mechanism guarantees
the safe finalization of a corrupted shard’s block once all
shards in an endorsement group have sequentially declared
their endorsements.
For example, in Figure 1, corrupted Shardi  is forked and
is required to eliminate the fork with the help of shards in the
endorsement group. Without  loss of generality,  the endorse-
ment group consists of Shardi, Shardi+1, and Shardi+2.
′  ′′
Shardi  is forked and produces blocks I1 and I1 via intra-
shard consensus. These blocks can be inherently considered as
receiving Shardi’s endorsement since passing the intra-shard
′′
consensus. However, only block I1  is sequentially endorsed
by Shardi+1’s block J. Consequently, block J is endorsed
′′
by Shardi+2’s block K,  thus original block I1  is endorsed
′′
by all shards in the endorsement group. Hence, only block I1
is finalized. In this case, the fork in Shardi is eliminated, and
′′
Shardi has to extend the chain after block I1 .
To further refine our liveness guarantee against the possible
liveness attack by corrupted shards (i.e., not generating any
blocks and halting the linked endorsement), we adopt a mixed
threat model where Byzantine nodes target both safety and
liveness while alive-but-corrupt nodes [36] (CCS’ 19) only
compromise safety but not liveness.

Under this mixed threat
model, we ensure that the fraction of Byzantine nodes in each
shard remains below 1/3, and we allow corrupted shards to
contain additional no more than 1/3 of a-b-c nodes. These
measures are guided by our rigorous theoretical analysis pre-
sented in Section V. As a result, the quorum size of votes can
be achieved to advance consensus within each shard, ensuring
the continuous extension of the linked endorsement.
3). How to maintain high efficiency when there are some
slow shards  in the system? In SpiralShard, we pipeline
the block generation  to enhance overall  throughput. Gen-.

A. Blockchain and Blockchain Sharding
Traditional blockchain systems require nodes to store and
verify every transaction on a single chain, resulting in poor
performance and scalability. For example, Bitcoin can only
process 7 tx/s (transaction per second), while Ethereum can
only process 15 tx/s [24]. Recent works adopt sharding [35]
to improve the scalability of blockchain [6], [17], [25], [29],
[31], [35], [38], [45], [46]. The main idea of sharding is to
partition the nodes into multiple small committees (shards).
Each shard maintains a disjoint subset of the whole blockchain
state, conducts intra-shard consensus, and processes distinct
transactions in parallel. However, existing blockchain sharding
systems still suffer from limited concurrency.
B. Shard Size and Concurrency
We find that the main reason for the poor concurrency of
the existing blockchain sharding systems is the large shard
size,  resulting  in slower  intra-shard consensus  and  fewer
shards. However, large shard sizes are set to limit the fraction
of malicious nodes  in each shard under  its fault  tolerance
threshold when the nodes are randomly assigned.
Some works  [17],  [21],  [23],  [29],  [35],  [46],  [47]  try
to improve system concurrency by reducing the shard size.
However,  they have various limitations. For  instance,  some
systems [23], [29], [35], [46], [47] tolerate a larger fraction

3
(i.e., < 1/2) of malicious nodes within each shard based
on a less practical assumption that  the network within each
shard is synchronous. However, real-world network environ-
ments are often not synchronous (e.g., partial-synchronous),
especially for large-scale blockchain systems. SGX-Sharding
[17]  reduces  the shard size by applying trusted hardware.
However,  it brings additional deployment overhead to each
node,  limiting  its generality. Some other  solutions  reduce
the shard size at  the cost of reducing the systems’ overall
resiliency, compromising the system’s security. For example,
in Pyramid [21], the assumption entails a smaller fraction of
malicious nodes in the network, resulting in a system capable
of withstanding less than 1/8 of malicious nodes.
Motivation and Intuition. Can we safely configure small
shard sizes, enhancing the transaction concurrency? Our in-
tuition is to allow some shards to be corrupted and leverage
honest shards to recover corrupted shards from forking.
C. Blockchain Sharding with Corrupted Shards
Only a handful of blockchain sharding studies allow cor-
rupted shards, with various limitations. Free2Shard [39] al-
lows corrupted shards and preserves  the system’s security
via a network-wide consensus. However,  it  is based on the
assumption of a global  synchronous network environment.
Therefore, it does not work correctly under a practical partial-
synchronous network.  In addition,  Free2Shard adopts  the
Proof of Work (PoW) consensus protocol, which does not
provide deterministic finality and is prone to forking prob-
lems. In GearBox [18], the system relies on a network-wide
consensus to ensure the system’s security, which can easily
lead to a performance bottleneck in large-scale networks. The
latest work, known as CoChain [32], similarly stems from an
intuition that allows shard corruption to enhance concurrency.
In CoChain, shards are randomly assigned to CoC (Consensus
on Consensus) groups. However, CoChain only permits the
failure of  less than one-third of  the shards within a group.
When the group size is no larger  than 3, CoChain cannot
allow any corrupted shard, losing feasibility. More importantly,
CoChain needs to rely on very time-consuming state migration
to replace the corrupted shards when they fork. In contrast,
our SpiralShard can utilize the LCE protocol  to easily help
corrupted shards remove excess forks without  the need for
shard replacement or recovery.
SpiralShard is a novel design that is different from existing
blockchain sharding systems. Built upon the assumption of
partial-synchronous networks, it is more practical. Leveraging
BFT-typed intra-shard consensus ensures finality and energy
efficiency. Unlike systems  reliant  on global  consensus  to
resolve forks, it leverages multiple endorsement groups, pre-
serving scalability. It requires at least one shard of each group
to be honest, offering greater flexibility in endorsement group
size.
D.  Interchain Timestamping
In the cross-blockchain context,  timestamping technology
enables  less secure blockchains  to harness  the security of

A. Network Model
SpiralShard works on a partial-synchronous Peer  to Peer
(P2P) network, in which each message will reach the receiver
within an unknown upper bound δ delay after being sent from
nodes [20]. Like most previous blockchain sharding systems,
the messages are propagated through gossip protocol  [19].
Each node in the system has its public/secret key pair, which
can be authenticated through a trusted Public-Key Infrastruc-
ture (PKI) to represent  its identity while sending messages.
Like many prior works [5], [21], [29], SpiralShard utilize a
beacon chain to publicly store the identities of participants.
In SpiralShard, the network consists of N nodes. The shard
size (i.e.,  the number of nodes per shard) and the endorse-
ment group size (i.e., the number of shards per endorsement
group) are denoted as S and G,  respectively. This implies
that  each endorsement  group contains S · G nodes, with
N/S shards within the network. A node’s endorsement group
identity is determined by its shard identity (i.e., GroupID =
⌊ShardID/G⌋).
B. Transaction Model
SpiralShard employs the account/balance transaction model,
widely adopted by existing works to represent ledger states [5],
[7],  [11],  [17],  [21],  [22],  [26],  [33],  [45].  In SpiralShard,
a specific account’s state (e.g.,  balance)  is maintained by
one shard, determined by the hash of  its account address.
Accordingly, a transaction in the network is routed to the cor-
responding shard based on its associated account address. It is
important to emphasize that this includes both intra-shard and
cross-shard transactions. Besides,  the two accounts involved
in cross-shard transactions may be in different endorsement
groups.
C. Threat Model
In SpiralShard, there are honest and malicious nodes. The
honest nodes obey all  the protocols. There are  two kinds
of malicious nodes: Byzantine and alive-but-corrupt  (a-b-c)
nodes [36]. The Byzantine nodes may collude and corrupt
the system arbitrarily, such as remaining silent, sending con-
flicting messages  to different nodes, and voting for blocks
containing  invalid  transactions. A-b-c nodes,  proposed by

4
Fig. 2.  System architecture.
Flexible BFT [36], are widely recognized as a typical and
rational type of attacker, especially in real-world permission-
less blockchains [15],  [27],  [28],  [36]. A-b-c nodes always
properly attempt  to sign and send inconsistent messages to
different nodes to break safety (e.g.,  fork chains to launch
double-spending attacks). However,  they positively vote for
proposals to push consensus forward. Align with the previous
work [36], a-b-c nodes should be treated as Byzantine during
the safety proof. And once we have proved safety, we can treat
a-b-c nodes as honest while proving liveness.
A-b-c nodes are considered a typical and rational  type of
attacker  [15],  [27],  [28],  [36],  especially in permissionless
blockchain systems. The rationale behind this stems from the
nature of blockchain payment systems, wherein some attackers
often benefit by compromising system safety, evidenced by
the substantial losses incurred due to double-spending attacks
in Bitcoin and Ethereum Classic [1], [3]. On the other hand,
nodes  are motivated  to preserve  liveness  to earn service
fees [15], [27], [28], [36], [40]–[42].
The fraction of Byzantine and a-b-c nodes in the system
are denoted as FB and FA. Thus, there are N · FB Byzantine
and N · FA a-b-c nodes in the system, respectively. Like most
blockchain systems, we assume that the adversaries are slowly-
adaptive, i.e., the set of malicious nodes and honest nodes are
fixed during each epoch (e.g., one day) and can be changed
only between epochs [34].

We  aim to design a blockchain  sharding  system that
achieves higher  concurrency. As  shown  in Figure 2, we
partition nodes  in the network into multiple small  shards,
each maintaining its own shard chain. However, some shards
may be corrupted due to their small size, compromising the
system’s security (e.g.,  fork chains  to launch double-spend
attacks). Our intuition is to leverage honest shards to verify
and endorse blocks on only one branch of forks in corrupted
shards. Hence, we establish the concept of the endorsement
group, which comprises multiple shards, including at least one
honest shard, as shown in Figure 2. For the sake of safety and
liveness, we have designed the LCE protocol that each shard
sequentially declares its endorsement. As shown in Figure 2,
the endorsements are linked to providing finality for ancestor
blocks.
The running of SpiralShard proceeds in fixed periods called
epochs. Like most existing sharding blockchain systems, we

′
Fig. 3.  Linked Cross-shard Endorsement for blocks A2.
set  the epoch duration  to one day  to  resist  the  slowly-
adapted adversary [17], [21], [46]. During each epoch, existing
blockchain sharding systems perform intra-shard consensus
within each shard to package transactions  into blocks and
securely append them to shard chains. However, since Spiral-
Shard configures more shards to boost transaction concurrency,
there may be corrupted shards  in SpiralShard due  to the
smaller  shard size. To  tackle  this  issue,  the main design
difference between our system and previous systems is the
Linked Cross-shard Endorsement protocol.
We first  give  the designs of  the LCE protocol  facing
different challenges. Specifically, we illustrate the basic design
of LCE preserving security in Section IV-A, how to opti-
mize cross-shard communication for the LCE process in Sec-
tion IV-B, and how to ensure the performance of the LCE pro-
tocol in Section IV-C. Subsequently, in Section IV-D, we in-
troduce other system components, including the bootstrapping
process, epoch transition, cross-shard transfer transactions, and
cross-shard smart contracts. While these components are not
the primary focus of our design innovations, they are integral
parts of blockchain sharding systems.
A. Basic Design of LCE
The LCE protocol requires that after a block is appended
to its shard chain via intra-shard consensus,  it must be se-
quentially endorsed by all shards in its endorsement group
before being finalized. To illustrate the process of a block,
from being appended to its shard chain to being finalized, the
LCE protocol declares three states for each block: 1) prepared,
2) finalized, and 3) discarded. A block that passes the intra-
shard consensus is considered prepared and would be finalized
if  it  is endorsed by all shards from its endorsement group
later. On the other hand, a prepared block that conflicts with
finalized ones is considered discarded.
The basic design requires transmitting blocks across shards
since they contain the history of each step of linked cross-shard
endorsement. However, in our final design, only transmitting
block headers is sufficient, reducing the cross-shard commu-
nication overhead. We will illustrate this later in Section IV-B.
The procedure of LCE includes three phases:

5
Block Preparation.  In SpiralShard,  each shard must  run
BFT-typed  intra-shard consensus  to append blocks  to  its
shard chain. We choose the Fast Byzantine Fault Tolerance
(FBFT)  [5]  consensus protocol  proposed by Harmony as
our  intra-shard consensus protocol. FBFT is a variant of
Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) [12], adopting a
short signature scheme, Boneh-Lynn-Shacham (BLS) multi-
signature [10]  to collect votes from participants for higher
scalability. Besides, similar to PBFT [12], FBFT has a review
change mechanism that relies on 2/3 of nodes’ participation
to replace the Byzantine leader of consensus. The intra-shard
consensus protocol is a pluggable component as long as it uses
a 2/3 quorum size and tolerates less than 1/3 Byzantine nodes
(e.g., PBFT [12]). Therefore, nodes can verify whether a block
has passed the intra-shard consensus through the signatures
in the block header. Such a block that passes the intra-shard
consensus is valid (does not conflict with the parent block)
and considered prepared and receives the endorsement  from
its shard. Honest nodes must broadcast  the prepared block
they vote for to the next shard for cross-shard endorsement.
An honest shard always appends only one prepared block to
the tail of its shard chain each time. However, when a shard
is corrupted, it can append multiple prepared blocks after the
same block, resulting in a fork.
′  ′′
As shown in Figure 3, blocks A2 and A2 are prepared
blocks,  forming a  fork  in shard0. Once  the blocks  are
prepared, nodes send the block they vote for to Shard1 for
being endorsed.

Then, we leverage Cross-shard Endorsement
and Block Finalization to eliminate the fork in Shard0.
Cross-shard Endorsement. The LCE protocol utilizes  the
endorsement group of multiple shards to decide on one of the
fork branches on a corrupted shard. Since an honest shard will
only endorse blocks on one fork branch on a corrupted shard
to eliminate forks, a group must contain at least one honest
shard with high probability. To ensure that multiple honest
shards within the same endorsement group make consistent
endorsement decisions, the LCE protocol requires that shards
conduct endorsements based on the ascending order of Shard
IDs.
In this phase, each shard is responsible for monitoring the

previous shard and making endorsement decisions via intra-
shard consensus. Specifically,  it verifies  the validity of  the
prepared block received from the preceding shard and selects
a prepared one. Then,  it conducts intra-shard consensus to
record this prepared block’s hash into its block header. Each
honest node will ensure that the endorsement decision in this
block does not conflict with that in its parent blocks (i.e., this
block and its parent blocks endorse on the same fork branch
from the preceding shard). Once such a block passes intra-
shard consensus,  it records the endorsement decision for its
shard.
For example, as shown in Figure 3, without loss of gener-
ality, an endorsement group consists of Shard0, Shard1, and
′  ′′
Shard2. After blocks A2 and A2 are prepared, Shard1 and
Shard2 need to determine their endorsements in succession. In
′  ′′
Shard1, block B3 can endorse either block A2 or A2 without
conflict because block B2 (parent of block B3) has endorsed
′  ′′
block A1  (parent of block A2 and A2 ). Without  loss of
generality, the proposer of block B3 in Shard1 receives valid
′  ′
prepared block A2 first, so Shard1 endorses block A2 in block
B3. In corrupted Shard2, the malicious block proposer forks
′  ′′
the shard chain by proposing two blocks (C4 and C4 ), both
of which can only endorse B3 after Shard1’s endorsement
decision.
Block Finalization. In this phase, each shard is responsible
for informing the finalized block back to its owner shard. A
prepared block will be finalized when it  is endorsed by all
shards in its endorsement group. Since the order of endorse-
ments is known, and the endorsement history is accumulated
and transmitted along the endorsement order,  the last shard
in the endorsement order knows  the original block that  is
fully endorsed.  In this case,  the last shard must  inform the
original shard of the finalized original block by transmitting
the accumulated endorsement history.
Once the original  shard selects  the block containing the
latest endorsement history from the last shard in the endorse-
ment order, the original shard’s nodes would confirm the pre-
execution of that finalized block and roll back (i.e., discard)
the conflicting prepared ones.
′
As shown in Figure 3, block A2 is prepared by Shard0 (i.e.,
endorsed by Shard0) and endorsed by block B3, then block
′  ′′  ′
B3  is endorsed by C4 or C4 . Block A2  is finalized since
it  is endorsed by all shards within the endorsement group.
′  ′′
Then, the honest nodes in Shard2 transmit block C4 or C4
containing the accumulated endorsement history to Shard0.
′
Note that the nodes in Shard0 can select either block C4 or
′′  ′
C4 since both finalize block A2. This safe finalization stems
from honest Shard1’s consistent endorsement of Shard0’s
′
blocks. Without loss of generality, Shard0 selects block C4,
′
then the nodes in Shard0 confirm block A2’s pre-execution
′′
and consider  the blocks from A2  to be discarded. Finally,
newly prepared blocks in Shard0 can only be the child of
′
block A2.
B. Efficient Cross-shard Communication for LCE
In  this part, we  illustrate how to reduce  the overhead
of cross-shard communication in LCE by only using block

6
Fig. 4.  Linked endorsements within an endorsement group of size M.
headers. Additionally, we only require block headers to be
broadcast to another shard rather than the entire network.
To verify the validity of a prepared block before endorsing,
a straw-man design is to store the shard state and verify each
raw transaction inside that block,  leading to massive over-
head. However, we hope to complete the verification before
endorsement with little overhead. To achieve this goal, we
fine-tune the system parameters according to the mathematical
expressions in Section V-A to ensure the fraction of malicious
nodes in each shard is less than 2/3, the quorum size of BFT-
typed consensus, with a high probability. Each prepared block
is valid since it has been verified and voted for by at least one
honest node in its shard. Moreover, we require each node to be
responsible for the cross-shard communication of the blocks
it votes for. Hence, at least one honest node will broadcast the
prepared block to the corresponding shard for subsequent en-
dorsement and finalization.

With the nodes’ identity recorded
in the beacon chain, each node from another shard only needs
to verify whether the received block is prepared by verifying
the signatures in the block header. Therefore, we reuse the
block header (instead of the whole block) with two additional
fields for endorsement and finalization. 1) The latest finalized
hash indicates the hash of the latest finalized block in its shard.
2) The endorsement  list consists of hashes of up to M − 1
blocks going back to the original shard’s block, indicating the
recent history of the linked endorsements. The endorsement
list  is updated across each step of the linked endorsements.
For example, when a shard proposes block B to endorse the
latest block A from the preceding shard in the endorsement
group, block B’s endorsement list is copied from that in block
A with an additional hash of block A.
Beyond leveraging block headers for endorsement and final-
ization, we underscore that each shard only needs to broadcast
its block headers to the next shard in the endorsement group
order rather than the entire network. The feasibility of it lies
in the fact that when a shard broadcasts a block header to the
subsequent shard,  the block header accumulates a history of
endorsements necessary to finalize a block of the subsequent
shard. Such a circular broadcasting within each endorsement
group inspires the name of our system, suggesting that  the
cross-shard endorsements within the endorsement group are
shaped like infinitely extending spirals, as shown in Figure 4.
In Figure 4, the links between blocks within the same shard are
hidden. Specifically, if Shardw has the largest shard ID in its
endorsement group of size M, its header would be sent to the
shard having the smallest shard ID within its group, otherwise,

Fig. 5.  Pipelining mechanism.
block headers of Shardw would be sent to Shardw+1.
C. Pipelining Mechanism
In SpiralShard,  the  sequentially processed endorsement
within the endorsement group may be halted due to a slow
shard. Hence, we pipeline block production so that a faster
shard can package transactions at its speed. This mechanism
requires shards to produce blocks optimistically and conduct
endorsement on the latest block from the preceding shard
instead of each block.
During the block preparation phase in LCE, a shard may
wait until its latest prepared block has been finalized before
preparing a new one. However,  in this case, once there is a
slow shard leading to a slow finalization, the speed of block
preparation of all  the shards within the same endorsement
group will be slowed down. Hence, we require  that  each
shard optimistically prepares blocks after non-discarded blocks
instead of directly after finalized blocks. After optimistic
preparation, nodes are still required to broadcast the prepared
blocks to the next shard. In this case, even if the blocks can
not be finalized in time, shards can continue to package new
transactions to the blocks.
Additionally, during the cross-shard endorsement phase in
1
LCE, a shard may receive multiple blocks at different heights
optimistically prepared by the preceding shard.  If a shard
needs to endorse each prepared block of the preceding shard
directly,  then the finalization speed for each shard is limited
by the slowest  shard within the same endorsement group.
Hence, we  require each shard  to endorse  the  latest  non-
conflicting prepared block from the preceding shard instead
of each block. Specifically,  the node verifies the validity of
this endorsement decision to prevent it from conflicting with
the parent block’s endorsement decision (i.e., endorse on the
different fork branches).
During the block finalization phase in LCE, the block A may
not directly collect sufficient endorsements. But it is also safely
finalized once block A’s subsequent block is finalized since
1
The ”height” of a block refers to the number of blocks that precede it in
the same chain. For instance, the first block in the chain has a height of 0,
and the next block has a height of 1, and so forth.

7
no other blocks conflicting with block A can be sufficiently
endorsed. As a result, shards can optimistically append blocks
to the chains and finalize blocks in time.
As  shown  in Figure 5, we set  the endorsement  group
consisting of Shard0, Shard1, and Shard2, where Shard1 is
slow. Shard2 optimistically produces blocks C2, C3, and C4
without waiting, and all of these blocks endorse the latest non-
conflicting block B1 from Shard1. Then Shard1 produces
block B2 endorsing the latest block A3 from Shard0. Once
block C5  is produced to endorse block B2,  block A3  is
endorsed by all  the shards within the endorsement group.
Block A1 and A2 can also be considered finalized since they
are parents of block A3. Honest nodes in Shard0 must show
the headers from block A2 to block A6 to prove that block A2
is a parent of finalized block A3 to finalize A2.

As a result,
once the slow shard prepares new blocks, optimistic prepared
blocks from other shards can be quickly finalized to restore
throughput.
D. Other Components
Beyond our main designs, we provide brief remarks about
implementing the other components, including bootstrapping,
shard reconfiguration, cross-shard transfer  transactions, and
cross-shard smart contracts.
First, before the first epoch, there is a one-time trusted boot-
strapping process, a reasonable assumption widely adopted
in research and industrial areas [5],  [29].  In practice, boot-
strapping can be carried out  in blockchain projects  in a
centralized [5] or decentralized way [46].
Second, since the adversary is slowly-adapted (i.e.,  it can
infect  some honest  nodes after  a while), we periodically
reshuffle shards to prevent aggregation of malicious nodes.
Between two epochs, SpiralShard has a shard reconfiguration
process (also existing in most existing works [5], [21], [46],
[47]) to determine members of each shard in the next epoch.
For security,  the nodes are randomly assigned to different
shards. So we leverage a combination of verifiable random
function (VRF) [37] and verifiable delay function (VDF) [9]
to generate verifiable, unbiased, and unpredictable distributed
randomness for this process, similar to that of other sharding
systems [21], [29], [46]. To prevent Sybil attack, SpiralShard
applies Proof of Stake (PoS) as the access mechanism for
nodes who want  to join the system. Similarly, blockchain
systems can use PoW as an access scheme [21], [46].
Third, similar to many existing works [5], [21], [29], there is
a beacon chain in SpiralShard that records the nodes’ identity
information and assists in shard reconfiguration. Note that the
beacon chain maintains the identity of participants for each
epoch, epoch randomness, and the starting block height of
each epoch, which assists the progress of LCE across epochs
and will be discussed later in Section V-C. Thus, the identity
of a node for each epoch is public.
Fourth, the cross-shard transfer transactions are processed
via the traditional relay-based mechanism [5],  [45].  In Spi-
ralShard, each cross-shard transfer  transaction involves two
accounts in different shards. Specifically,  source shards are
responsible  for  initially packaging cross-shard  transactions.

A. Epoch Security
In this section, we calculate the upper bound of the failure
probability within each epoch.  In SpiralShard, we leverage
cumulative hypergeometric distribution function to calculate
probabilities, similar to previous works [21], [29], [31], [46],
[47]. Besides, previous works  typically assume  the failure
probability of each shard is independent while adopting union

8
2
bound  to calculate the upper bound of the system’s failure
probability [21],  [29],  [31],  [46],  [47]. We adopt  the same
assumption and mathematical  tools.  In SpiralShard, an en-
dorsement group will fail in three cases: Case 1: It contains
no honest shard, i.e., all shards in this endorsement group have
at least 1/3 fraction of malicious nodes. Case 2: It contains
honest shards, but at least one shard is corrupted by at least
2/3 fraction of malicious nodes. Case 3: It contains honest
shards, but at  least one shard is corrupted by at  least 1/3
fraction of Byzantine nodes.
We first calculate an endorsement group’s failure probability
for case 1. Here, we calculate the probability of the event that
the proportion of malicious nodes in an endorsement group
is at  least 1/3 as the upper bound since this is a necessary
condition for such an endorsement group not to contain any
honest shard. Note that the network size,  the shard size,  the
endorsement group size, and the fraction of malicious nodes
in the network are denoted as N, S, G, F (F = FA + FB),
respectively. Correspondingly, we denote the number of nodes
per endorsement group as M = S · G for  simplicity of
formulas. Let Xi denote the random variable of the number
of malicious nodes in endorsement group i. The probability
of endorsement group i having Xi = x malicious nodes is:

B. Protocol Security Analysis
Based on negligible epoch failure probability, we now ana-
lyze the safety and liveness of our LCE protocol. Specifically,
safety indicates that honest nodes agree on the sequence of the
same and valid finalized blocks in each shard chain. Liveness
indicates each shard continuously has blocks being finalized.
Thus,  the system progresses, similar to other permissionless
sharding systems [29], [46]. We first analyze the safety of the
LCE protocol.
Theorem 1. The LCE protocol provides safety if each cor-
rupted shard has less than 2/3 malicious nodes within the
same epoch and each endorsement group has less than 1/3 of
malicious nodes within the same epoch.
Proof. Without loss of generality, we demonstrate the safety
of a specific finalized block Ba. Since block Ba is finalized, it
has been verified by at least one honest node and has received
endorsements from all shards in its endorsement group within
the epoch. Assuming another block, Bb, which conflicts with
Ba, is finalized in the same epoch. This assumption is based
on two distinct scenarios. Firstly, all shards in the endorsement
group simultaneously endorse these two blocks. In this case,
the proportion of malicious nodes in the endorsement group
must be at least 1/3, as each shard within the group is cor-
rupted by at least 1/3 fraction of malicious nodes. However, it
contradicts the assumption that the fraction of malicious nodes
within each endorsement group is less than 1/3. Secondly,
a corrupted shard manipulates the other shards’ endorsement
decisions by setting an invalid endorsement  list  in its block
header. This requires the number of malicious nodes within
this corrupted shard to be larger than the quorum size of BFT-
typed consensus. However, it contradicts the assumption that
the fraction of malicious nodes within each shard is less than
2/3.  In both scenarios,  the assumptions of  the theorem are
violated. Thus, if the conditions of the theorem are met, the
safety is preserved without losing generality.
We first prove that the intra-shard consensus is continuously
reached in Lemma 1,  similar  to the proof of  liveness  in
Flexible BFT [36]. Based on this, we then prove that the LCE
protocol can preserve liveness.
Lemma 1. The intra-shard BFT consensus is continuously
reached if there are less than 1/3 fraction of Byzantine nodes
plus no more than 1/3 fraction of a-b-c nodes for each shard
and at least one honest shard within each endorsement group.
Proof.  In accordance with the definition of a-b-c nodes, elabo-
rated in Section III-C and supported by the existing work [36],
these nodes preserve  liveness.  Indeed,  as demonstrated in
Theorem 1, safety is provided if each endorsement group has at
least one honest shard and no shard corrupted by at least 2/3
of malicious nodes. Consequently, when proving Lemma 1
and once safety is established,  the a-b-c nodes would not
prevent intra-shard consensus being reached aligning with the
principles of a-b-c nodes in the existing work [36]. In addition,
BFT-typed consensus protocols typically have a view-change
mechanism that  relies on the participation of 2/3 nodes to
change leaders when no consensus is reached (e.g., PBFT [12].

A.  Implementation and Experimental Setup
We implement a prototype of SpiralShard in Golang based
on Harmony [5],  a well-known permissionless blockchain
sharding project once with top 50 market cap in cryptocur-
rency. We choose FBFT [5]  consensus protocol  proposed
by Harmony as our intra-shard consensus protocol. Such a
choice is made for 1) simplifying the implementation process
and 2) providing a fair evaluation to show the performance
gains from the main ideas of SpiralShard.

(a) Total TPS  (b) Confirmation latency
Fig. 6.  Performance comparison under various network sizes.
We deploy a network with up  to 4, 200 nodes on 21
Amazon EC2 instances, each having a 96-core CPU and up
to 20 Gbps network bandwidth. We  limit  the network by
using Docker container and Linux Traffic Control (TC). Each
node has 50 Mbps individual network bandwidth and 100 ms
latency for each message. We set the transaction size as 512
bytes and let each block contain up to 4, 096 transactions.
Moreover, we generate transactions based on historical data
of Ethereum [48], where cross-shard transactions’ proportion
increases with  the number  of  shards. The  system’s  total
fraction of malicious nodes is set to 1/4, including 1/8 fraction
of Byzantine nodes and 1/8 fraction of a-b-c nodes.
B. Parameter Settings
In SpiralShard,  the endorsement  group and shard sizes
are the primary system parameters that are fine-tuned while
running.  In our  implementation, we leverage an authorized
account (controlled by developers) that can publicly set one
of these parameters in the beacon chain. Note that the setting
can be decentralized by using a Decentralized Autonomous
Organization (DAO). When one of the two parameters is deter-
mined, each node can individually adjust the other parameter
from small  to large until a negligible failure probability is
obtained to get  the exact  result. We require the calculated
−17  −6
upper bound to be less than 2  ≈ 7.6×10  , meaning that
the system fails once in 359 years,  the same as the existing
systems [21], [31].
In the baseline, we set the shard size under the negligible
failure probability of the system according to the classical hy-
pergeometric distribution function (e.g., the equations in [46]),
as the baseline does not have our LCE design.
The parameter settings are shown in Table I. By default, we
choose the shard size of around 100 for SpiralShard, which
is practical  and used by some previous works  [17],  [21],
[23], [29], [46]. According to Table I, SpiralShard significantly
reduces the shard size under the negligible failure probability,
increasing the number of shards under the same network size.
As shown in Table II, we provide a reference to update group
sizes with increased network sizes when developers have set
different shard sizes. For example, given a shard size of 100
and network sizes, the system follows the second column to
set endorsement group sizes. Additionally, when the network
scale  is fixed, we recommend that developers of  latency-
sensitive systems increase the shard size to reduce the size
of endorsement groups, thereby achieving the goal of trading
throughput for lower latency (see Section VI-E).

C. Throughput
We compare the average throughput in terms of transactions
per second (TPS) of SpiralShard and the baseline at different
network sizes. As shown in Figure 6a, SpiralShard achieves
more than 9.5× throughput. Moreover, SpiralShard achieves
up to 19.5× throughput gain at a network size of 3,000. The
main reasons behind this are  three-fold. First, SpiralShard
achieves reduced shard size, resulting in a faster intra-shard
consensus. Second, the larger number of shards in SpiralShard
brings higher  transaction concurrency. Third,  the pipelining
mechanism allows each shard to continuously produce blocks
without being halted by the slower shards.
D. Latency
We compare the average transaction confirmation latency
of SpiralShard and the baseline under different network sizes.
Transaction confirmation latency here is  the duration from
when a transaction starts to be processed until the transaction is
confirmed, the same as Monoxide [45].

As shown in Figure 6b,
SpiralShard has longer latency (up to 1.9× at a network size of
4,200). By comparing Figure 6a and Figure 6b, we find that
although the throughput of SpiralShard is greatly improved,
the transaction confirmation latency is not too large. The main
reason is that, although SpiralShard requires each block to
be sequentially endorsed by other shards,  the significantly
reduced shard size speeds up intra-shard consensus, shortening
the latency for each step of the LCE, resulting in an acceptable
overall latency.
E. Performance under Various Shard Sizes
In SpiralShard, we can configure multiple combinations of
shard and endorsement group sizes flexibility under negli-

As shown in Figure 7a,  the overall  throughput of Spiral-
Shard decreases as the shard size increases. This is because
the increased shard size slows the intra-shard consensus and
reduces the number of shards,  threatening the system’s con-
currency. However, SpiralShard still has better throughput. For
example,  even if  the shard size is 300,  the throughput of
SpiralShard is still 3.8 × higher  than that of  the baseline.
For the baseline protocol,  the shard size cannot be changed
arbitrarily to meet the system’s success probability at the same
network size, so its throughput remains the same.
The transaction confirmation latency is shown in Figure 7b.
Somewhat counterintuitively, as the shard size increases,  the
transaction confirmation latency in SpiralShard also decreases.
The main reason is  that although the increased shard size
slows down the shard consensus,  it decreases  the size of
each endorsement group. As a result, a block only needs to
be endorsed by fewer shards,  leading to lower  transaction
confirmation latency.

G. Performance with More Malicious Nodes
We evaluate the performance of SpiralShard in the presence
of 1/3 fraction of total malicious nodes (with 15% fraction
of Byzantine nodes) under the 800 network size. The results
are shown in Table V. Specifically, with such a high fraction
of total malicious nodes, the baseline can only configure one
shard to ensure the system’s security, while SpiralShard can
configure 8 shards. More importantly, SpiralShard achieves
49x higher throughput  than the baseline protocol. However,
the latency of SpiralShard is higher. The main reason is that
SpiralShard adopts a large endorsement group, extending the
latency for a block to be finalized.

H. Performance with a Slow Shard
In this experiment, we evaluate the performance of Spiral-
Shard in the presence of one slow shard under the configura-
tion shown in Table V. Specifically, we partition the network

(a) Total TPS  (b) Confirmation latency
Fig. 10.  Performance during an epoch transition.
inside a shard for a while to simulate the situation where
a shard cannot perform consensus due to network problems
during a certain period, similar to previous work [43].
As illustrated in Figure 9, we start  the network partition
in a shard at 75s and continue for 60s. It is shown that  the
number of finalized blocks gradually decreases during this
period.

The reason is that since our LCE protocol requires se-
quential endorsement between shards within the endorsement
group, a slow shard will halt the LCE’s processing within the
endorsement group. This results in a decreasing number of
blocks that can be finalized. Additionally, to fully reflect the
impact of slow shard within the endorsement group, we have
configured only one endorsement group. After  the network
partition period,  the consensus process for  that  slow shard
resumes, and the number of finalized blocks increases rapidly.
This is because, in the LCE protocol, when the newest block
collects enough endorsements, all of it and its parent blocks
are finalized. Therefore, those blocks not finalized during the
network partition period will be finalized together rapidly after
the consensus is restored.
I. Performance During an Epoch Transition
In this section, we evaluate the performance during an epoch
transition. Specifically,  as  the system consistently finalizes
blocks, we trigger an epoch transition at T = 5. As shown in
Figure 10a, there is a sharp drop in the throughput following
the onset of  the epoch transition. This  is primarily due to
state synchronization conducted by nodes before joining new
shards. The subsequent peak in throughput stems from the new
epoch’s finalizing prepared blocks from the previous epoch.
As observed in Figure 10b,  there is a peak in latency after
the conclusion of  the epoch transition. The main reason is
that blocks generated toward the end of an epoch necessitate
sequential endorsement from scratch in the new epoch. These
findings underscore that system performance experiences an
impact during epoch transitions. However, since epoch transi-
tions occur only once daily, aligning with existing works [21],

.
